docs.title: Opal RM prototype documentation
docs.text:
This site is the published version of the repository onboarding guide for hmcts/opal-rm-prototype.

Use it if you need to:

  • set up a Windows laptop from scratch
  • set up a MacBook from scratch
  • learn the basic Git commands needed for day-to-day work
  • understand how to use Codex to make prototype changes safely
  • document the Search journey flows and matching logic for functional specification work
  • understand how the repository publishes documentation previews for branches and pull requests

docs.title: Start here
docs.text:
If you are new to development tools, read in this order:

  1. Windows setup or Mac setup
  2. Git and GitHub basics
  3. Using Codex for prototyping
  4. Troubleshooting

docs.title: Functional documentation
docs.text: • Search flows and logic

docs.title: What this repository is
docs.text:
This repository contains a GOV.UK Prototype Kit prototype for an Opal RM case creation journey.

It is a prototype, not a production service. That means:

  • speed and clarity matter more than heavy engineering patterns
  • there is no persistent backend or database
  • data is stored in the session only

docs.title: Useful local commands
docs.text:
Install dependencies:

npm install

Run the prototype locally:

npm run dev

Open the prototype:

http://localhost:3000

docs.title: Published documentation URLs
docs.text:
When the GitHub Pages workflow runs successfully:

  • latest docs from main: https://hmcts.github.io/opal-rm-prototype/latest/
  • branch preview: https://hmcts.github.io/opal-rm-prototype/branches/<branch-name>/
  • pull request preview: https://hmcts.github.io/opal-rm-prototype/pulls/pr-<number>/

docs.title: Git and GitHub basics
docs.text: This page covers the small set of commands most people need every day.

docs.title: The safest daily workflow
docs.text:
Start from the latest main:

git checkout main
git pull --ff-only origin main

Create your own branch:

git checkout -b docs/my-change

Check what changed:

git status
git diff

Stage files:

git add README.md docs/

Commit:

git commit -m "docs: improve onboarding guide"

Push the branch for the first time:

git push -u origin docs/my-change

Later pushes:

git push

docs.title: Useful commands
docs.text:
See your current branch:

git branch --show-current

Download new branches from GitHub:

git fetch origin

Switch to an existing branch:

git checkout branch-name

Show recent commit history:

git log --oneline --decorate -10

Discard an uncommitted change in one file:

git restore path/to/file

docs.title: How to raise a pull request
docs.text:
1. Push your branch.
  2. Open GitHub in the browser.
  3. Click Compare & pull request.
  4. Add a clear title and summary.
  5. Create the pull request.

For branches created inside this repository, the docs workflow publishes:

  • a branch preview
  • a pull request preview

The pull request gets a comment with the preview link.

docs.title: GitHub Pages publishing
docs.text:
This repository has two separate delivery paths:

  1. Azure App Service deployment for the prototype application
  2. GitHub Pages deployment for documentation

They are intentionally separate.

docs.title: What gets published
docs.text:
The documentation workflow publishes to three stable URL patterns:

  • latest/ for the current main branch documentation
  • branches/<branch-name>/ for branch previews
  • pulls/pr-<number>/ for pull request previews

Examples:

  • https://hmcts.github.io/opal-rm-prototype/latest/
  • https://hmcts.github.io/opal-rm-prototype/branches/readme_update/
  • https://hmcts.github.io/opal-rm-prototype/pulls/pr-123/

docs.title: One-time GitHub setup
docs.text:
A maintainer must do this once:

  1. Open the repository in GitHub.
  2. Go to Settings -> Pages.
  3. Choose Deploy from a branch.
  4. Select the gh-pages branch.
  5. Select the / (root) folder.
  6. Save the setting.

docs.title: Local preview for maintainers
docs.text:
If you want to preview the docs site locally before pushing:

python -m venv .venv
. .venv/bin/activate
python -m pip install -r docs/requirements.txt
mkdocs serve

Then open:

http://127.0.0.1:8000

docs.title: Safety rule for pull requests
docs.text:
PR previews are only published when the PR branch lives inside this repository.

Fork pull requests are excluded on purpose so the publishing workflow does not grant write-capable deployment behavior to untrusted branches.

docs.title: Why this repository uses gh-pages
docs.text:
This repository uses a workflow that writes static files to the gh-pages branch.

That approach is intentional because it allows multiple versions of the documentation to coexist at the same time:

  • latest/
  • branches/<branch-name>/
  • pulls/pr-<number>/

That is what makes branch and PR preview URLs possible without waiting for a merge to main.

docs.title: MacBook setup from scratch
docs.text:
These instructions assume:

  • you are using a new or reset MacBook
  • you have not set up Git before
  • you want a simple, repeatable setup

docs.title: Before you install anything
docs.text:
Make sure you have:

  1. Access to GitHub.
  2. Access to the hmcts/opal-rm-prototype repository.
  3. Permission to install software.
  4. A normal working folder, for example ~/Projects.

Do not keep the repository in iCloud Drive.

docs.title: Xcode Command Line Tools
docs.text:
Open Terminal and run:

xcode-select --install

docs.title: Homebrew
docs.text:
/bin/bash -c "$(curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Homebrew/install/HEAD/install.sh)"

If Homebrew prints extra commands to add it to your shell profile, run them before continuing.

Check Homebrew:

brew --version

docs.title: Git and Visual Studio Code
docs.text:
brew install git
brew install --cask visual-studio-code

Check Git:

git --version

docs.title: Node.js 24 with nvm
docs.text:
brew install nvm
mkdir -p ~/.nvm

Resolve the correct Homebrew path first. This works on both Apple Silicon and Intel Macs:

NVM_PREFIX="$(brew --prefix nvm)"

Add this to ~/.zshrc:

export NVM_DIR="$HOME/.nvm"
export NVM_PREFIX="$(brew --prefix nvm)"
[ -s "$NVM_PREFIX/nvm.sh" ] && \. "$NVM_PREFIX/nvm.sh"
[ -s "$NVM_PREFIX/etc/bash_completion.d/nvm" ] && \. "$NVM_PREFIX/etc/bash_completion.d/nvm"

Restart Terminal, then run:

nvm install 24
nvm use 24
node --version
npm --version

docs.title: Set your Git identity and safe defaults
docs.text:
git config --global init.defaultBranch main
git config --global pull.ff only
git config --global core.autocrlf input
git config --global core.eol lf
git config --global user.name "Your Full Name"
git config --global user.email "your.name@justice.gov.uk"

docs.title: Set up SSH for GitHub
docs.text:
Create the key:

mkdir -p ~/.ssh
ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -C "your.name@justice.gov.uk" -f ~/.ssh/id_ed25519_hmcts

Create the HMCTS SSH alias:

cat <<'EOF' > ~/.ssh/config
Host github.com-hmcts
  HostName github.com
  User git
  IdentityFile ~/.ssh/id_ed25519_hmcts
  AddKeysToAgent yes
  IdentitiesOnly yes
EOF

Copy the key:

pbcopy < ~/.ssh/id_ed25519_hmcts.pub

Add it in GitHub under Settings -> SSH and GPG keys.

Test access:

ssh -T git@github.com-hmcts

docs.title: Clone and run the repository
docs.text:
mkdir -p ~/Projects
cd ~/Projects
git clone git@github.com-hmcts:hmcts/opal-rm-prototype.git
cd opal-rm-prototype
npm install
npm run dev

Open:

http://localhost:3000

docs.title: First-time success checklist
docs.text:
You are ready if all of these are true:

  • git --version works
  • node --version works
  • npm --version works
  • ssh -T git@github.com-hmcts works
  • npm run dev starts the prototype

docs.title: REMO Active Account Usability Testing Script
Session Setup
docs.text:
Prototype URL: http://localhost:3050

Start each task from the Search page unless the participant is already on a relevant account page. Ask the participant to think aloud. Do not explain the navigation unless they are blocked and ask for help.

Use the sample data in this script only when the participant needs to enter new or changed information. The search criteria are chosen from records that exist in the prototype.

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text:
Thank you for taking part. We are testing the prototype, not you. Some things may not work as you expect, and that is useful for us to learn.

Please say what you are thinking as you work through each task. For example, what you are looking for, what you expect to happen, and anything that feels unclear.

docs.title: Warm-Up
docs.text:
Before we start the tasks, please take a moment to look at the Search page.

Prompts:

  • What would you do first if you needed to find an account?
  • Is anything unclear on this page?

docs.title: Task 1: Edit Respondent Details, Authority, Arrears Date, And Comment
Participant Task
docs.text:
Find the active account for Cem Demir.

On the respondent account, update the third party correspondence address. Then change the central authority, update the date arrears were last updated, and add a comment to the file.

docs.title: Search Criteria
docs.text:
Use one of these:

  • Respondent last name: DEMIR
  • Respondent first names: Cem
  • Account number: 26000060C

Expected account:

  • Respondent account: 26000060C
  • Name: DEMIR, Cem
  • Case type: REMO Out

docs.title: Sample Data To Enter
docs.text:
Third party correspondence address:

  • Name or organisation: keep as Demir & Partners Solicitors
  • Relationship: keep as Solicitor
  • Reference: keep as CD/2024/001
  • Address line 1: Level 2
  • Address line 2: 14 West Street
  • Address line 3: Brighton
  • Address line 4: East Sussex
  • Postal or zip code: BN1 2RE
  • Country: United Kingdom

Central authority:

  • Select central authority: Central Authority - Australia
  • Central authority reference: AUS-CSA-2026-387

Order details:

  • Date arrears last updated: 15/06/2026

Comment:

Third party correspondence address updated. Central authority changed following new information from Australia. Arrears date updated from latest schedule.

docs.title: Success Criteria
docs.text:
The participant can:

  • Find the respondent account from Search.
  • Locate where respondent third party details are edited.
  • Locate where authority details are edited.
  • Locate order details and update the arrears date.
  • Add or change the account comment.
  • Recognise whether each change has been saved.

docs.title: Moderator Prompts
docs.text:
• What made you choose that tab or link?
  • Was it clear whether this was respondent, applicant, or order information?
  • Did the success message reassure you that the change was saved?

docs.title: Task 2: Edit Applicant Address And Add Third Party Address
Participant Task
docs.text: Find the active account for Alina Popa. Open the applicant account. Change the applicant address and add a third party correspondence address.

docs.title: Search Criteria
docs.text:
Use one of these:

  • Applicant last name: POPA
  • Applicant first names: Alina
  • Applicant account number: 26000511A

Expected account:

  • Applicant account: 26000511A
  • Name: POPA, Alina
  • Linked respondent: POPA, Andrei

docs.title: Sample Data To Enter
docs.text:
Applicant address:

  • Address line 1: 22 Willow Court
  • Address line 2: Newbury
  • Address line 3: Berkshire
  • Postal or zip code: RG14 5QP
  • Country: United Kingdom

Third party correspondence address:

  • Select that correspondence should be sent to a third party.
  • Name or organisation: West Berkshire Family Law
  • Relationship: Solicitor
  • Reference: AP/POPA/2026
  • Address line 1: 3 Market Place
  • Address line 2: Newbury
  • Address line 3: Berkshire
  • Postal or zip code: RG14 5AA
  • Country: United Kingdom

docs.title: Success Criteria
docs.text:
The participant can:

  • Find the applicant account from Search.
  • Understand that the applicant account opens separately from the respondent account.
  • Find the applicant edit journey.
  • Update the applicant address.
  • Add third party correspondence details.
  • Save and return to the applicant account.

docs.title: Moderator Prompts
docs.text:
• Did you expect the applicant account to open in a new tab?
  • Was it clear where to edit applicant details?
  • Was the third party correspondence section easy to find?

docs.title: Task 3: Change Minor Creditor Name And Add Note
Participant Task
docs.text: Find the active account for the minor creditor Mira Popa. Change the minor creditor name and add a note explaining why the name has changed.

docs.title: Search Criteria
docs.text:
Use one of these:

  • Minor creditor last name: POPA
  • Minor creditor first names: Mira
  • Minor creditor account number: 26000522C

Expected account:

  • Minor creditor account: 26000522C
  • Name: POPA, Mira
  • Linked respondent: POPA, Andrei

docs.title: Sample Data To Enter
docs.text:
Minor creditor name:

  • First names: Mira Elena
  • Last name: POPA

Account note:

Minor creditor first names updated to match updated birth certificate provided by the applicant.

docs.title: Success Criteria
docs.text:
The participant can:

  • Find a minor creditor using Search.
  • Identify that they are on a minor creditor account.
  • Find where to edit creditor details.
  • Change the name.
  • Add an account note.
  • Check that the note is available in history and notes.

docs.title: Moderator Prompts
docs.text:
• Was it clear this was a minor creditor rather than the applicant or respondent?
  • Did you know where to add the reason for the name change?
  • Did the history and notes area behave as expected?

docs.title: Participant Task
docs.text: Find and view the details for the major creditor linked to the Demir case.

docs.title: Search Criteria
docs.text:
Use:

  • Major creditor: Central Authority - Australia

Expected account:

  • Major creditor account: 26000623R
  • Name: Australian Child Support Agency
  • Case reference: 06000387W

docs.title: What To Observe
docs.text:
Ask the participant to tell you:

  • The major creditor name.
  • The address shown.
  • Whether BACS details have been provided.
  • The amount awaiting payout.

Expected details:

  • Name: Australian Child Support Agency
  • Address: Level 3, Centrepoint Tower, Sydney, NSW 2000, Australia
  • BACS details: PROVIDED
  • Awaiting payout: £180.00

docs.title: Success Criteria
docs.text:
The participant can:

  • Find the major creditor search field.
  • Search using the major creditor criterion.
  • Recognise they have landed on a major creditor account.
  • Find the main details and payout status.

docs.title: Moderator Prompts
docs.text:
• Was it obvious how to search for a major creditor?
  • Did the result page provide enough context about the linked case?
  • What else would you expect to see on a major creditor account?

docs.title: Wrap-Up Questions
docs.text:
• Which task felt easiest?
  • Which task felt hardest?
  • Were any labels or tabs unclear?
  • Did opening account numbers in new tabs help or get in the way?
  • Was it clear when you were editing respondent, applicant, minor creditor, order, authority, or comment information?
  • What would you change before this was tested with more users?

docs.title: Notes Template
docs.text:
Participant:

Date:

Moderator:

Prototype version or branch:

Task 1 notes:

Task 2 notes:

Task 3 notes:

Task 4 notes:

Overall observations:

docs.title: Search flows and logic
docs.text: This page documents the Search journey in the prototype for functional specification work. It covers the user flows, search criteria, matching rules, result handling, and prototype-specific notes for simple search and advanced search.

docs.title: Scope
docs.text:
The Search journey lets a user find cases and accounts, then open the relevant respondent, applicant, minor creditor, or major creditor account.

Search is available from /search and is shown as the active Search item in the service navigation.

The current prototype uses in-memory prototype data. It does not call a backend, database, or external search service.

docs.title: Screen Flow
docs.text:
flowchart TD
  A["Search page"] --> B["User enters simple search criteria"]
  A --> C["User opens advanced search and enters advanced criteria"]
  B --> D["Submit search"]
  C --> D
  D --> E{"Simple and advanced criteria both entered?"}
  E -->|Yes| F["Conflicting criteria screen"]
  E -->|No| G{"No searchable criteria entered?"}
  G -->|Yes| A
  G -->|No| H{"Major creditor only?"}
  H -->|Yes| I{"Major creditor account found?"}
  I -->|Yes| J["Major creditor account page"]
  I -->|No| K["No results screen"]
  H -->|No| L["Run case and account search"]
  L --> M{"Number of matches"}
  M -->|0| K
  M -->|More than 100| N["Too many results screen"]
  M -->|1 to 100| O["Search results page"]
  O --> P["Open respondent, applicant, or minor creditor account"]

docs.title: Search Page
docs.text:
The main search page is titled "Search for cases and accounts".

Simple search is shown first. Advanced search is hidden in a GOV.UK details component labelled "or use advanced search". Advanced search uses tabs for:

  • Respondents
  • Applicants
  • Minor creditors
  • Major creditors

There is also an "Active accounts only" checkbox in the advanced search section. It is checked by default in the interface.

docs.title: Visible criteria
docs.text:
The visible simple search field is:

Field Purpose Account number Find a case by respondent, applicant, minor creditor, or major creditor account number.

docs.title: Supported server-side criteria
docs.text:
The route also supports a reference-number field, although that field is not currently visible on the search page template. The conflict screen describes this as "a reference or case number".

If the reference number field is posted, it searches against:

  • REMO reference
  • case reference

docs.title: Simple search matching rules
docs.text:
Account number search:

  • must match an account number exactly after normalising spaces and hyphens
  • checks respondent account numbers, applicant account numbers, minor creditor account numbers, and major creditor account numbers
  • uses account prefixes generated by the prototype:
  • RP for respondent accounts
  • AP for applicant accounts
  • MC for minor creditor accounts
  • MA for major creditor accounts

Reference number search:

  • matches against the REMO reference or case reference
  • is case-insensitive
  • ignores spaces and hyphens
  • supports partial matching

docs.title: Advanced Search
docs.text: Advanced search lets the user search using respondent, applicant, minor creditor, or major creditor details.

docs.title: Respondent fields
docs.text: Field Matching rule Last name Case-insensitive partial match First names Case-insensitive partial match Date of birth Exact date match after normalisation Postal or zip code Partial compact match, ignoring spaces and hyphens

docs.title: Applicant fields
docs.text: Field Matching rule Last name Case-insensitive partial match First names Case-insensitive partial match Date of birth Exact date match after normalisation Postal or zip code Partial compact match, ignoring spaces and hyphens

docs.title: Minor creditor fields
docs.text:
The minor creditor tab has a radio choice for "Individual" or "Company".

Individual fields:

Field Matching rule Last name Case-insensitive partial match First names Case-insensitive partial match Postal or zip code Partial compact match, ignoring spaces and hyphens

Company fields:

Field Matching rule Company name Case-insensitive partial match Postal or zip code Partial compact match, ignoring spaces and hyphens

For a minor creditor search, the case must have at least one minor creditor that matches all the minor creditor criteria entered.

docs.title: Major creditor field
docs.text:
The major creditor tab uses an autocomplete control with central authority options, for example "Central Authority - Australia".

If a major creditor is the only advanced search criterion:

  • the prototype looks for a major creditor account with that major creditor code
  • if found, the user is taken directly to the major creditor account page
  • if not found, the user is taken to the no results screen

If a major creditor is combined with other advanced search criteria:

  • it acts as another filter
  • the case must match the other advanced criteria and have a major creditor account with the selected major creditor code

docs.title: Combining Criteria
docs.text:
The user must search using either simple search criteria or advanced search criteria, not both.

If the user enters both:

  • an account number or reference number, and
  • any respondent, applicant, minor creditor, or major creditor field

they are shown the conflicting criteria screen.

Within advanced search, criteria are combined using "and" logic:

  • if respondent fields are entered, all entered respondent fields must match
  • if applicant fields are entered, all entered applicant fields must match
  • if minor creditor fields are entered, one minor creditor on the case must match all entered minor creditor fields
  • if a major creditor is selected with other criteria, the case must also have that major creditor
  • if "Active accounts only" is selected, the case status must be Active

The active accounts checkbox does not count as a searchable criterion on its own. If it is the only selected option and no search fields are entered, the user is returned to the search page.

docs.title: Normalisation Rules
docs.text:
Before matching, the prototype normalises some values:

Value type Normalisation General text Trimmed and converted to lowercase Compact text, such as account numbers and postcodes Trimmed, lowercased, and spaces and hyphens removed Dates Normalised to DD/MM/YYYY where possible

Accepted date formats include:

  • YYYY-MM-DD
  • D/M/YYYY or DD/MM/YYYY
  • written dates such as 15 March 1975

Dates must match exactly after normalisation.

docs.title: Results
docs.text:
If the search returns no matches, the user is shown the no results screen.

If the search returns more than 100 matches, the user is shown the too many results screen and asked to narrow the search.

If the search returns between 1 and 100 matches:

  • the matching rows are stored in session data
  • the user is redirected to the search results page
  • the results table shows:
  • respondent account
  • applicant account
  • minor creditor accounts
  • status
  • arrears
  • respondent, applicant, and minor creditor account numbers are links to their account pages
  • if there are more than 25 results, MOJ pagination is shown
  • each results page shows 25 results

docs.title: Error And Exception Screens
docs.text: Scenario Destination User-facing message Simple and advanced criteria are both entered /search/conflicting-criteria "You cannot search using a quick search field and advanced search fields at the same time" No matching results /search/no-results "There are no matching results." More than 100 matching results /search/too-many-results "There are more than 100 results. Narrow your search to see fewer results." No criteria entered /search The user is returned to the search page.

docs.title: Prototype Data Notes
docs.text:
Search runs against the compact searchData list in app/routes.js. That data is also used to populate respondent and creditor account records for the account pages.

The current prototype data includes:

  • respondent accounts
  • applicant accounts
  • minor creditor accounts
  • one major creditor account for Central Authority - Australia

The minor creditor company search path exists in the route logic, but the current compact search data contains individual minor creditor records rather than company minor creditor records. Unless company minor creditor data is added, company searches are likely to return no results.

The route reads address line 1 fields for respondent, applicant, and minor creditor searches, but those address line 1 fields are not currently displayed on the search page. The visible address-related fields are postal or zip code fields.

docs.title: Implementation References
docs.text:
• Search page template: app/views/search/index.html
  • Search results template: app/views/search/results.html
  • Search exception templates: app/views/search/conflicting-criteria.html, app/views/search/no-results.html, app/views/search/too-many-results.html
  • Search route and matching logic: app/routes.js, search section from // -- Search
  • Major creditor autocomplete options: majorCreditorOptions in app/routes.js
  • Major creditor prototype account data: majorCreditorAccounts in app/routes.js

docs.title: git clone says permission denied
docs.text:
Check:

ssh -T git@github.com-hmcts

If it fails:

  1. Confirm your public key was added to GitHub.
  2. Confirm ~/.ssh/config contains the github.com-hmcts host alias.
  3. Confirm the repository access was granted to your GitHub account.

docs.title: node or npm is not recognised
docs.text:
Your terminal cannot find Node.js.

Fix:

  1. Close and reopen the terminal.
  2. Re-run your Node setup steps.
  3. Check:
node --version
npm --version

docs.title: npm install fails
docs.text:
Try:

npm cache verify
npm install

If it still fails:

  1. Make sure you are in the repository root.
  2. Make sure your internet connection is stable.
  3. Make sure you installed Node 24, not an older version.

docs.title: npm run dev starts but the browser page does not load
docs.text:
Check the terminal for the local URL and open it manually:

http://localhost:3000

If it still does not load:

  1. Stop the server with Ctrl+C.
  2. Start it again with npm run dev.
  3. Confirm nothing else is already using port 3000.

docs.title: I am on the wrong branch
docs.text:
Check your branch:

git branch --show-current

Switch to the branch you want:

git checkout branch-name

docs.title: I changed files and want to keep them safe before switching branch
docs.text:
Commit them before switching:

git add .
git commit -m "wip: save current work"

If you do not want to commit yet, use git stash only if someone on your team has shown you how it works.

docs.title: The docs preview did not appear on my pull request
docs.text:
Check these points:

  1. The PR must come from a branch in this repository, not a fork.
  2. GitHub Pages must be enabled in Settings -> Pages using Deploy from a branch, with gh-pages and / (root) selected.
  3. The workflow run must be green in the Actions tab.

docs.title: Using Codex for prototyping
docs.text: This guide is for business analysts and designers who want to use Codex to help change the prototype.

docs.title: How to start Codex for this repository
docs.text:
Start Codex from the repository root so it can inspect the right files.

  1. Open Terminal on Mac or Git Bash on Windows.
  2. Move into the repository folder:
cd ~/Projects/opal-rm-prototype
  1. Check you are in the right folder:
pwd
git status
  1. If this is your first use on that machine, sign in:
codex login
  1. Start the interactive Codex session:
codex
  1. Paste one of the prompts from this guide.
  2. Review the files Codex changed with git status and git diff.
  3. Run the prototype with npm run dev and check the result in your browser.

If the terminal says codex is not recognised, stop there and ask your team how Codex is installed on your HMCTS laptop before going further.

docs.title: What Codex is good at in this repository
docs.text:
Codex is useful for:

  • adding a new page to the prototype journey
  • changing wording on existing pages
  • moving or adding journey steps
  • replacing custom markup with GOV.UK or MOJ components
  • improving page structure so it is easier to test with users
  • explaining which files control a screen or a journey

Codex is less useful if your request is vague.

Good:

  • "Add an interpreter needs page after hearing details."
  • "Change the review screen so it shows a warning when the email is missing."
  • "Use GOV.UK radios instead of a select on the case type page."

Weak:

  • "Improve this."
  • "Make it better."
  • "Do something modern."

docs.title: How this prototype is structured
docs.text:
These are the files Codex will usually need to change:

  • app/views/ for page templates
  • app/routes.js for navigation and flow logic
  • app/data/session-data-defaults.js for default answers or stored session values
  • app/filters.js for template filters
  • app/assets/javascripts/application.js for small front-end behaviour
  • app/assets/sass/application.scss for small style changes

docs.title: Rules Codex should follow here
docs.text:
This repository is a GOV.UK Prototype Kit prototype, so the safest instructions are:

  • use GOV.UK Design System and MOJ Design System components
  • prefer Nunjucks macros instead of hand-written HTML
  • keep styling changes small
  • keep the repository as a prototype, not a production application
  • do not add a backend, database, or API layer unless explicitly asked

docs.title: Add a page
docs.text: Add a new page to this prototype after the hearing details step. The page should ask whether an interpreter is required. Use GOV.UK radios, save the answer in session data, and update app/routes.js so the journey continues to the review page.

docs.title: Change page content
docs.text: Update the review page so the applicant and respondent details are easier to read. Use GOV.UK summary lists and clear headings.

docs.title: Ask for an explanation first
docs.text: Explain this prototype repository to me as a business analyst. Show me which files I would change for page content, flow logic, and styling.

docs.title: Make a page more GOV.UK-aligned
docs.text: Refactor this page to use GOV.UK or MOJ macros where possible. Keep the prototype simple and easy to change.

docs.title: Add a warning or condition
docs.text: If no respondent email has been entered, show a GOV.UK warning text on the review page and explain which file you changed.

docs.title: Best way to work with Codex
docs.text:
1. Say what page or journey step you want to change.
  2. Say what the user should see or be able to do.
  3. Say whether the answer should affect the next page.
  4. Ask Codex to explain which files it changed.

docs.title: A simple BA or designer workflow
docs.text:
1. Run the prototype locally with npm run dev.
  2. Open the current page in the browser.
  3. Write down what you want to change.
  4. Give Codex a precise instruction.
  5. Run the prototype again and check the result in the browser.
  6. If needed, ask Codex for a refinement.

docs.title: If you are not sure where to start
docs.text:
Use this:

I am a business analyst working on this GOV.UK prototype. Explain the journey structure and then make the smallest change needed to add a new question page before the review page.

If you want a safer first run, use this instead:

I am new to Codex and this repository. First explain which files control the page content, the flow logic, and the styling. Then make one very small prototype-safe change and tell me exactly which files changed.

docs.title: Windows setup from scratch
docs.text:
These instructions assume:

  • you are using an HMCTS-managed Windows laptop
  • you have not set up Git before
  • you want the safest path with the fewest surprises

docs.title: Before you install anything
docs.text:
Check that you have:

  1. Access to GitHub.
  2. Access to the hmcts/opal-rm-prototype repository.
  3. Permission to install software.
  4. A normal working folder, for example C:\Users\<your-name>\Projects.

Do not clone the repository into OneDrive or a shared network folder.

docs.title: Visual Studio Code
docs.text:
1. Go to https://code.visualstudio.com/.
  2. Download the Windows installer.
  3. Install it using default options.

docs.title: Git for Windows
docs.text:
1. Go to https://git-scm.com/download/win.
  2. Download and run the installer.
  3. Keep the default options unless your team tells you otherwise.
  4. Open Git Bash when the install finishes.

Check Git:

git --version

docs.title: Node.js 24
docs.text:
Recommended approach:

  1. Install nvm-windows from https://github.com/coreybutler/nvm-windows/releases.
  2. Open a new Git Bash window.
  3. Run:
nvm install 24.0.0
nvm use 24.0.0
node --version
npm --version

If nvm-windows is blocked, install Node 24 directly from https://nodejs.org/.

docs.title: Set your Git identity and safe defaults
docs.text:
Open Git Bash and run:

git config --global init.defaultBranch main
git config --global pull.ff only
git config --global core.autocrlf input
git config --global core.eol lf
git config --global user.name "Your Full Name"
git config --global user.email "your.name@justice.gov.uk"

docs.title: Set up SSH for GitHub
docs.text:
Create the key:

mkdir -p ~/.ssh
ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -C "your.name@justice.gov.uk" -f ~/.ssh/id_ed25519_hmcts

Create the HMCTS SSH alias:

cat <<'EOF' > ~/.ssh/config
Host github.com-hmcts
  HostName github.com
  User git
  IdentityFile ~/.ssh/id_ed25519_hmcts
  AddKeysToAgent yes
  IdentitiesOnly yes
EOF

Copy the public key:

cat ~/.ssh/id_ed25519_hmcts.pub | clip

Then add it in GitHub under Settings -> SSH and GPG keys.

Test access:

ssh -T git@github.com-hmcts

docs.title: Clone and run the repository
docs.text:
mkdir -p ~/Projects
cd ~/Projects
git clone git@github.com-hmcts:hmcts/opal-rm-prototype.git
cd opal-rm-prototype
npm install
npm run dev

Open the prototype:

http://localhost:3000

docs.title: First-time success checklist
docs.text:
You are ready if all of these are true:

  • git --version works
  • node --version works
  • npm --version works
  • ssh -T git@github.com-hmcts works
  • npm run dev starts the prototype
